1: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying: The standard prophetic address formula (waydabber YHWH el-Moshe le'mor) frames what follows not as legislation improvised after the event but as the divine interpretation of the Exodus itself. The consecration command is the theological corollary to the tenth plague.
2: Consecrate to me (qadeshu-li) every firstborn (kol-bechor): Qadash (to set apart as holy) is the priestly consecration verb; bechor derives from bakar (to be first, to bear firstfruit). Petter rechem (opener of the womb) — from pathar (to open/burst through) — specifies the firstborn as the one who ruptures the birth canal, the biological and legal first of the line. Both human and animal firstborn are addressed; the command grounds the Passover logic: Israel's firstborn were spared because a substitute died in their place.
3: Remember this day (zachor et-hayom hazeh): Zachor is the covenant-memory verb, used in Deuteronomy for sabbath (5:15), for the Exodus itself (16:3), and for the Amalekite attack (25:17). It means more than intellectual recall — it means to make present and act on. House of slaves (beit avadim): Egypt is defined not by geography but by its social structure; Israel is defined as the people freed from it.
4: Month of Abib (chodesh ha-aviv): Aviv = green/tender ears of grain (barley ripening); the month is named from agricultural phenology, not a number or a god. Later calendar reform (Babylonian influence) replaced aviv with Nisan, but Deuteronomy still uses aviv (16:1). The spring month marks both the agricultural new year and the redemptive new beginning.
5: When YHWH brings you (ki yeviacha): The causal-temporal ki + Hiphil imperfect of bo' anticipates the land-gift as already settled. Flowing with milk and honey (zavet chalav u'devash): The stock formula (appears ~20 times in the Pentateuch) describes agricultural abundance — chalav (milk from flocks) and devash (honey from date palms or wild bees). The five nations named are the standard catalog of Canaan's pre-Israelite inhabitants.
6: Seven days you shall eat matzot (unleavened bread): Seven is the week of completeness; the festival (chag) on the seventh day is a mirror of the Sabbath structure embedded in Passover observance. The temporal overlap of the week-festival and the annual commemoration links personal discipline (no leaven in the house) to communal celebration.
7: No leaven shall be seen with you (lo-yera'eh lecha chametz): The Niphal of ra'ah (to be seen) used as passive; chametz = leavened dough; se'or = sourdough starter or leaven. The prohibition covers not just eating but possession throughout all your territory (kol-gevulecha). Paul's midrash on this text (1 Cor 5:7-8) treats leaven as a metaphor for corruption spreading through a community.
8: You shall tell your son (v'higadeta l'vincha): The Hiphil of nagad (to declare/tell) is the catechetical verb for Passover explanation. B'avur zeh (because of this, on account of this) — the demonstrative this points to the physical matzot and bitter herbs currently before the narrator; memory is made tangible through objects. The Passover Haggadah (from this same root nagad) derives its name from this verse.
9: Sign (l'ot) on your hand... memorial (l'zikaron) between your eyes: Ot = sign (same term as circumcision in Gen 17:11, rainbow in Gen 9:12-13) — a physical marker of covenant status. Zikaron = memorial/reminder from zakar (the memory verb of v.3). The parallel is later literalized as tefillin (Deut 6:8 uses totafot): leather boxes with Torah portions worn on arm and forehead. Torah of YHWH in your mouth: The sign-on-body motif points to the ultimate goal — scripture internalized as speech.
10: Statute (chuqqah) at its appointed time (moed): Chuqqah = binding ordinance from chaqaq (to engrave/inscribe); it conveys permanence — the law engraved, not merely spoken. Moed = appointed time/meeting, the same root as ohel moed (tent of meeting); both the tabernacle entrance and the annual feasts are moadim — times of divine-human rendezvous.
11: When YHWH brings you to the land of the Canaanite: The promise-fulfillment frame (ki + Hiphil imperfect of bo') recurs from v.5, creating a sandwich structure around the Passover regulations. The firstborn law (vv.11-16) extends the Passover redemption logic into the future life of the nation in the land.
12: You shall cause to pass over (ha'avarta) to YHWH all that opens the womb: The Hiphil of avar (to cross/pass over) uses the Passover verb — peh samech chaf — the same root as Pesach; the firstborn are 'passed over' to YHWH just as death 'passed over' Israel's houses. Every firstborn male (zachar) of your cattle belongs to YHWH (laYHWH).
13: Firstborn donkey (petter chamor) redeemed (tifdeh) with a lamb (seh): The donkey (chamor) is the paradigm unclean animal — it cannot be sacrificed on the altar, so it must either be redeemed by substitution (padah = ransom by exchange) or have its neck broken (va'arafto — from oref, the nape of the neck). The lamb stands in for the donkey; the substitution logic is explicit. Padah (to redeem by payment/substitution) is distinct from ga'al (to redeem as kinsman); both appear in the Exodus redemption vocabulary. Every firstborn son also requires redemption — human sons and unclean animals share the same status before YHWH: too precious/dangerous to be unredeemed.
14: When your son asks in time to come (machar): Machar = tomorrow/future; the catechetical question-and-answer sequence (asked also in Exod 12:26; Deut 6:20; Josh 4:6) structures Passover transmission across generations. By strength of hand (bechozek yad) YHWH brought us out: The yad (hand) is the primary Exodus metaphor — the outstretched arm, the strong hand. The summary formula 'hand + Egypt + slaves' condenses the whole narrative into a sentence for children.
15: Pharaoh hardened himself (hikshaih): Here the root is qashah (to be hard/difficult), distinct from chazaq (strengthen) and kaved (be heavy) used elsewhere. The three hardening roots together form a theological composite: the heart can be made stubborn (qashah), strengthened in its resolve (chazaq), or weighted down in dullness (kaved). Pharaoh's hardening is both self-induced and divinely confirmed. YHWH killed all the firstborns: The theological consequence: because Pharaoh's firstborn died, Israel's firstborn must be consecrated — the covenant reciprocity of the tenth plague.
16: Totafot (frontlets) between your eyes: Totafot is a hapax legomenon in the Pentateuch (also Deut 6:8, 11:18); the etymology is uncertain — possibly Akkadian tutuptu (band), possibly a foreign loanword. The rabbis identified these as the phylacteries (tefillin), small leather boxes containing Torah portions, bound to the arm and forehead during morning prayer. Whether the original command was meant literally or metaphorically is debated; the practice is ancient and attested in Second Temple Judaism.
17: God did not lead them by way of the land of the Philistines (derekh eretz-Pelishtim): The Philistines (Pelishtim) occupied the coastal plain (the Via Maris); archaeologically attested Egyptian military fortifications lined this route. Pen yinnachem (lest they regret/change their minds): Nacham = to be comforted/consoled, but also to change one's mind or regret — the divine rationale for the longer wilderness route reveals pastoral psychology: YHWH reads the people's fragility and accommodates it.
18: Yam Suph (Sea of Reeds): Suph = papyrus reeds/rushes (cf. 2:3 — the basket among the suph); the Greek translation 'Red Sea' (Erythra Thalassa) is a geographical overlay from later Hellenistic usage that identified the body of water with the Gulf of Suez. The Hebrew name points to a marshy reed-sea environment. Chamushim (armed/by fives?): The word appears twice in the OT (here and Josh 1:14); the root is debated — possibly chamesh (five, in military formation?) or chamush (loins/equipped). The LXX reads 'in the fifth generation' (fifth generation after the patriarchs?); most translations opt for 'armed for battle.'
19: Moses took the bones of Joseph (atzamot Yosef): Joseph had sworn Israel under oath (shevet hishbia = made them swear a solemn oath, Gen 50:25) to carry his bones out of Egypt. Atzamot = bones — the embodied continuity of the patriarchal promise; the living carry the dead through the wilderness into the land. The bones of Joseph, the greatest of Jacob's sons, travel with the redeemed nation as a sign that the covenant spans generations and crosses death.
20: Succoth... Etham: Succoth (Sukkot = booths/shelters) is probably Tell el-Maskhuta in the eastern Delta; Etham is at the edge of the wilderness (qetze hamidbar = end/border of the wilderness). The itinerary moves from Egyptian territory toward the desert frontier.
21: Pillar of cloud (ammud anan) by day... pillar of fire (ammud esh) by night: Ammud = column/pillar; anan = cloud; esh = fire. The double form — cloud (concealment + shelter from desert heat) and fire (warmth + light in darkness) — manifests both the hiddenness and accessibility of the divine presence. Lanchootam (to lead them on the way): Hiphil infinitive of nachah (to guide/lead) — the same verb for the divine leading in Ps 23:3 (yancheini b'ma'aglei tzedeq) and Isa 40:11. YHWH is Israel's shepherd-guide.
22: He did not remove (lo-yamish) the pillar: Yamish = Hiphil of mush (to remove/withdraw from a place); the negative verb emphasizes the constancy of the divine presence — day and night (yomam v'lailah) without interruption. The pillar is not intermittent but permanent throughout the wilderness journey, a sustained Shekinah-presence before the tabernacle formalizes the arrangement.
